era_context: The early 15th century continued with the Hundred Years' War in its later phases, the early Italian Renaissance in full development, the Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor's successors, and the Hussite Wars in Bohemia. The Ottoman Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Empire was consolidating power in the Valley of Mexico.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English maintain dominance in northern France
events.description: English forces under the Duke of Bedford maintained their military dominance in northern France in 1425, controlling Normandy and much of the north. The English were at the height of their power in France, with strong administrative and military control. This period of English dominance would be reversed by Joan of Arc's campaigns beginning in 1429.
key_figures: John, Duke of Bedford (English regent in France)
events.certainty_note: English dominance in northern France in 1425 is well-documented in English and French sources.

events.title: Zheng He's sixth voyage continues in the Indian Ocean
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's sixth treasure voyage continued in 1425, exploring the Indian Ocean and establishing diplomatic relations with various states. The voyage had departed in 1421 and would return to China in 1428. Zheng He's voyages demonstrated Ming naval power and established China as a major maritime power in the Indian Ocean.

sources.name: Ma Huan, Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's sixth voyage is well-documented in Ming archives. The voyage dates (1421–1428) are established in official records.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Continued military campaigns in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars continued in 1425 with ongoing military campaigns by Hussite forces against Catholic and imperial armies. The Hussites, a religious reform movement, maintained their military dominance in Bohemia despite opposition from Sigismund of Hungary and other Catholic powers. The conflict remained unresolved, with neither side achieving decisive victory.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Hussite Wars in 1425 is well-documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Brunelleschi's dome construction at Florence Cathedral progresses
events.description: Filippo Brunelleschi continued construction of the dome of Florence Cathedral in 1425, advancing his innovative engineering techniques. The dome was progressing toward completion, which would be achieved in 1429. Brunelleschi's work was attracting attention as a demonstration of Renaissance engineering and architectural innovation.
events.certainty_note: The progress of dome construction in 1425 is documented in Florence Cathedral's building records.

events.title: Aztec Empire consolidates power in the Valley of Mexico
events.description: The Aztec Empire, centered in Tenochtitlan, continued to consolidate its power in the Valley of Mexico in 1425. The city-states of Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, and Tlacopan were forming the Triple Alliance, which would become the foundation of Aztec imperial power. Military campaigns were expanding Aztec dominion over neighboring city-states and establishing a tribute system.

sources.name: Aztec oral tradition, recorded by Spanish chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Aztec consolidation in 1425 is inferred from post-conquest codices. The exact chronology of Aztec expansion in the 1420s is uncertain by ±1–2 years.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of African coast continues under Henry the Navigator
events.description: Portuguese maritime exploration of the West African coast, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued in 1425. Portuguese caravels were pushing further south along the African coast, establishing trading posts and gathering knowledge of African geography, peoples, and resources. This period marked the beginning of the Portuguese Atlantic slave trade, though the scale was still limited.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and trading records
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1425 is attested in royal records and later chronicles, though specific voyages and dates for individual expeditions in this year are less precisely documented than later 15th-century voyages.
cross_references: 1444-001 (First large-scale Portuguese slave raid on African coast)
1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed in 1425. The year is relatively sparsely documented compared to 1429, and most events are continuations of longer-term processes (Hundred Years' War, Hussite Wars, dome construction, Aztec consolidation, Portuguese exploration).
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1425 is moderate and heavily weighted toward European sources. Chinese sources for Zheng He's voyages are detailed and contemporary. However, Aztec history relies entirely on post-conquest indigenous codices interpreted through Spanish colonial frameworks. The Hussite Wars are well-documented in German, Bohemian, and Hungarian sources. African, Oceanic, and other non-literate societies are largely absent from surviving written records. The year 1425 is less densely documented than 1429, with fewer specific events recorded. Portuguese exploration is documented in royal records and later chronicles, but specific details for individual expeditions in 1425 are sparse.

graph_edges.note: English dominance in 1425 was reversed by Joan of Arc's campaigns in 1429
Aztec consolidation in 1425 led to the formalization of the Triple Alliance in 1428
